Austin Rope Slingers describes itself as a volunteer-run, donation-supported peer rope group focused on in-person peer-to-peer rope education. It offers a weekly 101-level class and higher-level education or rope intensives as members request and offer them.
At events, community resources include loaner rope for floor work, safety shears and straps, rope books, and negotiation reference guides. Labs also usually have suspension rigs offered by community members.

active_answer: They should establish what each person expects, including the kind of scene they want and any hard limits.
active_question: What should both parties clearly establish before a rope or BDSM session starts?
reason: The retained same-document row covers scene expectations and hard limits while adding the concrete instruction to ask explicitly rather than rely on uncertain nonverbal cues.
support_evidence: At the very least, I hope this will prompt us all to consider our actions more carefully and lead to creating a safer environment on both sides of the rope. The key word is clarity. Both parties need to be very clear about what they expect from a session, whether it involves rope or not, before it starts. The more doubt we can remove, the less likely it is for unintended transgressions to occur, with the inevitable trial by lynch mob, or for it to be used as a stock excuse by abusers. That said, we need workable solutions and not unrealistic criteria that nobody can be expected to meet every time. A good start, whilst by no means exhaustive, is asking “What sort of scene do you want? What hard limits do you have?”. I’m hoping Gorgone’s resource and the various threads on my Fetlife group will come up with some better advice.
